text.title: Agency-to-SaaS Readiness Checklist Core Rules
text.summary: Five hard rules for the agency-to-SaaS readiness audit.

section.title: The five dimensions
section.code:
D1 IP rights to candidate SaaS 2: Agency-owned IP, no client claims, no contract-bound derivative restrictions 1: Mixed — some SOWs allow derivative, some don't; manageable carve-out path 0: Client-owned IP on the candidate work; pivot requires re-build from scratch
D2 Repeatable IP (shipped same shape ≥ 3 times to ≥ 3 clients with similar scope) 2: Yes, ≥ 3 ships, ≥ 70% scope overlap 1: 2 ships OR 3+ ships with < 50% overlap (closer to template than product) 0: 0-1 ships of the candidate shape (no evidence of repeatability)
D3 Codebase ownership 2: All candidate code lives in agency-owned repos with permissive internal license 1: Mixed — some in client repos but with extraction rights 0: Code lives in client repos; extraction requires client signoff or rewrite
D4 Retainer-base stability 2: ≥ 9 months of runway under retainer-churn scenario (top-1 client leaves) 1: 6-9 months runway under same scenario 0: < 6 months runway; pivot threatens agency survival
D5 Founder bandwidth / replacement plan 2: Designated agency-ops lead in place; founder can spend 30+ h/w on SaaS 1: Plan exists but lead not yet hired; founder bandwidth limited to ~20 h/w 0: Founder is sole operator; no realistic replacement plan
Total score → decision ≥ 8/10 → READY (proceed to MVP) 5-7/10 → PRE-WORK NEEDED (close gaps before pivot) ≤ 4/10 → NOT VIABLE (revisit after 6+ months)

rule.statement: Every readiness audit scores ALL 5 dimensions on 0/1/2. No "skip" or "later" — undated unknowns score 0. The dimension list is fixed; do not add a 6th to soften a known weak dimension.

rationale.source: multi-criteria decision analysis; B2B pivot research (Constantin Bjerke 2023)

rule.rationale: Founders systematically over-weight their strongest dimension and under-weight the weakest. The 5-dimension mandatory scoring forces honest assessment of weak areas. Unknown scoring as 0 prevents "we'll figure it out" optimism.

rule.statement: Dimension D1 (IP rights) requires reading every SOW from the past 24 months for IP / derivative / "work-for-hire" / "all deliverables" language. Score is evidence-backed by a per-SOW table; cannot be assigned on memory.

rationale.source: agency-to-product pivots case studies; SoftwareEquity Group 2024 acquirer due-diligence checklist

rule.rationale: Agencies routinely sign SOWs with "all deliverables become client property" without realizing it bars productization. Acquirers / investors / customers will ask. Reading the contracts is the only valid evidence. Memory and "I think we own it" are 0 evidence.

rule.statement: Dimension D2 (repeatable IP) requires ≥ 3 shipped instances to ≥ 3 different clients with ≥ 70% scope overlap to score 2. The "we've kind of done this before" pattern scores 0.

rationale.source: lean-startup market validation; Jobs To Be Done switcher data

rule.rationale: Agencies often build a custom solution multiple times but with no scope overlap — that's a service, not a product. The 70% overlap threshold is the line between "we have a productizable kernel" and "we have a category of work." Below threshold, the SaaS would be re-inventing from scratch.

rule.statement: Dimension D4 (retainer stability) requires a 9-month runway under a stress test that assumes top-1 retainer client leaves at month 1. Pivots fail because the agency can't sustain itself through the SaaS build period.

rationale.source: startup runway analysis; agency exit case studies

rule.rationale: 9 months is the minimum SaaS build window for a meaningful MVP + early traction. Anything shorter means panic re-engaging with agency work mid-pivot, which kills both bets. The top-1 churn assumption is realistic — concentration is correlated with pivot announcements (existing client may churn when they sense divided focus).

rule.statement: Dimension D5 requires a NAMED operational lead for the agency during the pivot (not "we'll hire one later"). Founder time freed must be ≥ 30 hours/week sustained.
rule.rationale: Founder time is the silent killer of pivots. Splitting 20 hours between agency + SaaS produces two sub-par outcomes. The replacement lead must be hired BEFORE pivot decision, not as a TBD. Lead in place = realistic 30+ h/w founder time on SaaS.

example.title: A ready agency (score 9/10)
example.code: D1 IP rights: 2 (every SOW signed since 2024 has derivative-rights clause) D2 Repeatable IP: 2 (shipped CRM-integration tool 7 times, 85% scope overlap) D3 Codebase ownership: 2 (extracted to agency monorepo; reuse rate 60%) D4 Retainer base: 2 (15 months runway under top-1-churn scenario) D5 Founder bandwidth: 1 (ops lead hired Q4'25, ramping; founder at ~25 h/w currently) Total: 9 — READY (proceed to MVP; address D5 gap with 60-day ramp plan)

example.title: A not-ready audit shortcut
example.code:
"We've been working in this space for 5 years and the founders are sharp. We're ready to pivot."
Problem: violates r1 (no dimension scoring), r2 (no SOW audit), r3 (no repeatable-evidence threshold), r4 (no runway stress test), r5 (no replacement plan). Repair: do the actual audit. Most "we're ready" stories score 4-6/10, which is pre-work, not ready.
example.note: Vibes-based readiness is the canonical failure pattern.
